essential_question: Is √72 already as simple as it can be — or is there an exact, smaller way to write it?
objective: Simplify a radical by factoring out its largest perfect-square factor, combine like radicals, and multiply two radicals together and simplify the result.

content.prompt: √49 = 7 exactly, a whole number. √72 is NOT a whole number — but before you learn any rule, guess: can part of √72 come out from under the root sign even though the whole thing can't? Try pulling out a 6 and see what's left underneath.

agenda.title: I do — Perfect Squares, Simplifying & Like Radicals

content.heading: Pull out the largest perfect square; combine only like radicals
bullets: A perfect square is a whole number whose square root is ALSO a whole number: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144…
To simplify √n, find the LARGEST perfect square that divides n evenly, split the radical in two, and take that square root: √72 = √(36×2) = √36 × √2 = 6√2.

Like radicals share the exact same radicand once fully simplified — only then can you add or subtract their coefficients: 3√2 + 5√2 = 8√2. Unlike radicals (different radicands) can never combine: 5√2 + 3√5 stays as two terms.
Sometimes a radical needs simplifying FIRST to reveal it's secretly like another: √8 = 2√2, so √8 + 3√2 = 2√2 + 3√2 = 5√2.
Multiplying radicals: numbers times numbers, radicals times radicals — √a × √b = √ab, e.g. (5√3)(6√5) = 30√15 — and simplify afterward if the result allows it.

content.heading: Step through radicands and watch the factorization update
content.note: On the sim, step through the preset radicands (or type your own) and watch the largest-perfect-square factor tree build itself down to the simplified form. Then use Check Yourself to type your own simplified answer and see if it's fully reduced. Switch to Combine mode and decide, before revealing, whether each pair of radical terms are like or unlike.

content.preview: Build session: arrange unit tiles into perfect squares and physically factor a radicand into a block plus a remainder.

success_criteria.text: I can simplify a radical by factoring out its largest perfect-square factor
I can tell whether two radical terms are like or unlike, and combine like radicals
I can multiply two radicals together and simplify the result

questions.prompt: The largest perfect square that divides evenly into 72 is…
questions.explain: 72 ÷ 36 = 2 exactly, and no larger perfect square (49, 64…) divides 72 evenly — so 36 is the one to pull out.
Like radicals must share the exact same radicand — both of these have 6 under the root, so their coefficients (2 and 9) can be combined.

questions.prompt: Simplify √48 by factoring out its largest perfect-square factor. Show the factor pair you used.
questions.explain: 16 is the largest perfect square dividing 48 evenly (48 ÷ 16 = 3); √16 = 4, leaving 4√3.

questions.prompt: Simplify: 7√5 − 2√5. Then explain in one sentence why you're allowed to combine them.
questions.answer: 7√5 − 2√5 = 5√5 — allowed because both terms are LIKE radicals; they share the exact same radicand (5), so only the coefficients out front combine.
questions.explain: Since the radicands already match (both 5), just subtract the coefficients: 7 − 2 = 5, keeping √5 unchanged.
Numbers times numbers: 3 × 5 = 15. Radicals times radicals: √2 × √8 = √16 = 4, a whole number — so the radical disappears completely, leaving 15 × 4 = 60.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has two legs of length 6 and 6. The Pythagorean idea (leg² + leg² = hypotenuse²) works out to a hypotenuse of exactly √72. Simplify √72 to its exact, simplified form.
questions.explain: 36 is the largest perfect-square factor of 72. Next lesson's Pythagorean theorem will hand you radicals like this one as EXACT side lengths — simplifying keeps the answer clean and exact instead of a rounded decimal.

questions.prompt: Exit: pick any whole number between 20 and 100 that is NOT already a perfect square, and simplify its square root — name the perfect-square factor you pulled out.
